These optimizations reduce the model from 2.47 GB to as little as 0.67 GB while maintaining word error rate (WER) within 1% absolute of the full-precision PyTorch baseline. Our recommended configuration, the int4 k-quant variant, achieves 8.20% average streaming WER across eight standard benchmarks, running comfortably faster than real-time on CPU with 0.56 s algorithmic latency, establishing a new quality-efficiency Pareto point for on-device streaming ASR.

AI.tldr: Systematic study identifies Nemotron Speech Streaming as best for CPU-only edge ASR; quantization reduces model from 2.47GB to 0.67GB while maintaining WER within 1% of baseline.
AI.motivation: Need for high-quality ASR on edge devices that jointly optimizes accuracy, latency, and memory footprint while operating entirely on CPU without GPU acceleration.
AI.method: Systematic empirical study of 50+ ASR configurations across encoder-decoder, transducer, and LLM-based paradigms; re-implemented streaming pipeline in ONNX Runtime with multiple quantization strategies (importance-weighted k-quant, mixed-precision, round-to-nearest) and operator fusion.
AI.result: Nemotron Speech Streaming identified as strongest candidate; quantization reduced model from 2.47GB to 0.67GB while maintaining WER within 1% of full-precision baseline; int4 k-quant variant achieves 8.20% average streaming WER with 0.56s algorithmic latency.
AI.conclusion: Established new quality-efficiency Pareto point for on-device streaming ASR with optimized CPU-only deployment that balances accuracy, latency, and memory constraints.

title: DVFace: Spatio-Temporal Dual-Prior Diffusion for Video Face Restoration
summary: Video face restoration aims to enhance degraded face videos into high-quality results with realistic facial details, stable identity, and temporal coherence. Recent diffusion-based methods have brought strong generative priors to restoration and enabled more realistic detail synthesis. However, existing approaches for face videos still rely heavily on generic diffusion priors and multi-step sampling, which limit both facial adaptation and inference efficiency. These limitations motivate the use of one-step diffusion for video face restoration, yet achieving faithful facial recovery alongside temporally stable outputs remains challenging. In this paper, we propose, DVFace, a one-step diffusion framework for real-world video face restoration. Specifically, we introduce a spatio-temporal dual-codebook design to extract complementary spatial and temporal facial priors from degraded videos. We further propose an asymmetric spatio-temporal fusion module to inject these priors into the diffusion backbone according to their distinct roles. Evaluation on various benchmarks shows that DVFace delivers superior restoration quality, temporal consistency, and identity preservation compared to recent methods. Code: https://github.com/zhengchen1999/DVFace.

AI.tldr: DVFace is a one-step diffusion framework for video face restoration that uses spatio-temporal dual-codebooks and asymmetric fusion to achieve high-quality, temporally consistent results efficiently.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion-based video face restoration methods rely heavily on generic diffusion priors and multi-step sampling, which limit facial adaptation and inference efficiency. There's a need for one-step diffusion that can achieve faithful facial recovery with temporal stability.
AI.method: Proposes DVFace with: 1) spatio-temporal dual-codebook design to extract complementary spatial and temporal facial priors from degraded videos, and 2) asymmetric spatio-temporal fusion module to inject these priors into the diffusion backbone according to their distinct roles.
AI.result: Evaluation on various benchmarks shows DVFace delivers superior restoration quality, temporal consistency, and identity preservation compared to recent methods.
AI.conclusion: DVFace successfully addresses the challenges of one-step diffusion for video face restoration by effectively leveraging spatio-temporal facial priors, achieving efficient and high-quality results.

title: CAVERS: Multimodal SLAM Data from a Natural Karstic Cave with Ground Truth Motion Capture
summary: Autonomous robots operating in natural karstic caves face perception and navigation challenges that are qualitatively distinct from those encountered in mines or tunnels: irregular geometry, reflective wet surfaces, near-zero ambient light, and complex branching passages. Yet publicly available datasets targeting this environment remain scarce and offer limited sensing modalities and environmental diversity. We present CAVERS, a multimodal dataset acquired in two structurally distinct rooms of Cueva de la Victoria, Málaga, Spain, comprising 24 sequences totaling approximately 335 GB of recorded data. The sensor suite combines an Intel RealSense D435i RGB-D-I camera, an Optris PI640i near-IR thermal camera, and a Velodyne VLP-16 LiDAR, operated both handheld and mounted on a wheeled rover under full darkness and artificial illumination. For most of the sequences, mm-accurate 6-DoF ground truth pose and velocity at 120 Hz are provided by an Optirack motion capture system installed directly inside the cave. We benchmark seven state-of-the-art SLAM and odometry algorithms spanning visual, visual-inertial, thermal-inertial, and LiDAR-based pipelines, as well as a 3D reconstruction pipeline, demonstrating the dataset's usability. %The dataset and all supplementary material are publicly available at: https://github.com/spaceuma/cavers.

AI.tldr: CAVERS dataset provides multimodal sensor data from natural karstic caves with mm-accurate ground truth poses, addressing the scarcity of public datasets for challenging cave environments.
AI.motivation: Natural karstic caves present unique perception challenges (irregular geometry, reflective wet surfaces, near-zero light, complex branching) that differ from mines/tunnels, but public datasets for such environments are scarce with limited sensing modalities and diversity.
AI.method: Collected 24 sequences (335 GB) in two structurally distinct rooms of Cueva de la Victoria, Spain, using Intel RealSense D435i RGB-D-I camera, Optris PI640i near-IR thermal camera, and Velodyne VLP-16 LiDAR, operated both handheld and on a wheeled rover under darkness and artificial illumination. Most sequences include mm-accurate 6-DoF ground truth pose/velocity at 120 Hz from an Optirack motion capture system installed inside the cave.
AI.result: Created comprehensive multimodal cave dataset with precise ground truth. Benchmarked seven state-of-the-art SLAM/odometry algorithms (visual, visual-inertial, thermal-inertial, LiDAR-based) and a 3D reconstruction pipeline, demonstrating dataset's usability for algorithm evaluation.
AI.conclusion: CAVERS dataset fills a critical gap by providing high-quality multimodal sensor data with accurate ground truth from challenging natural cave environments, enabling development and benchmarking of perception and navigation algorithms for autonomous cave exploration.

title: Improving Machine Learning Performance with Synthetic Augmentation
summary:
Synthetic augmentation is increasingly used to mitigate data scarcity in financial machine learning, yet its statistical role remains poorly understood. We formalize synthetic augmentation as a modification of the effective training distribution and show that it induces a structural bias--variance trade-off: while additional samples may reduce estimation error, they may also shift the population objective whenever the synthetic distribution deviates from regions relevant under evaluation. To isolate informational gains from mechanical sample-size effects, we introduce a size-matched null augmentation and a finite-sample, non-parametric block permutation test that remains valid under weak temporal dependence.
  We evaluate this framework in both controlled Markov-switching environments and real financial datasets, including high-frequency option trade data and a daily equity panel. Across generators spanning bootstrap, copula-based models, variational autoencoders, diffusion models, and TimeGAN, we vary augmentation ratio, model capacity, task type, regime rarity, and signal-to-noise. We show that synthetic augmentation is beneficial only in variance-dominant regimes, such as persistent volatility forecasting-while it deteriorates performance in bias-dominant settings, including near-efficient directional prediction. Rare-regime targeting can improve domain-specific metrics but may conflict with unconditional permutation inference. Our results provide a structural perspective on when synthetic data improves financial learning performance and when it induces persistent distributional distortion.

AI.tldr: Synthetic augmentation in financial ML creates a bias-variance trade-off: helps in variance-dominant regimes but hurts in bias-dominant ones, with rare-regime targeting offering conditional benefits.
AI.motivation: To understand when synthetic data augmentation actually improves financial machine learning performance versus when it causes distributional distortion, since current understanding is poor despite widespread use.
AI.method: Formalize augmentation as modifying training distribution, introduce size-matched null augmentation to isolate effects, develop block permutation test for temporal data, evaluate across generators (bootstrap, copula, VAE, diffusion, TimeGAN) with varied augmentation ratios, model capacities, tasks, regime rarity, and signal-to-noise.
AI.result: Synthetic augmentation helps only in variance-dominant regimes (e.g., persistent volatility forecasting) but hurts in bias-dominant settings (e.g., near-efficient directional prediction). Rare-regime targeting improves domain metrics but conflicts with unconditional inference.
AI.conclusion: Provides structural framework for when synthetic data improves financial learning versus causes distortion, highlighting the critical bias-variance trade-off and conditional nature of augmentation benefits.

title: Revisiting Token Compression for Accelerating ViT-based Sparse Multi-View 3D Object Detectors
summary: Vision Transformer (ViT)-based sparse multi-view 3D object detectors have achieved remarkable accuracy but still suffer from high inference latency due to heavy token processing. To accelerate these models, token compression has been widely explored. However, our revisit of existing strategies, such as token pruning, merging, and patch size enlargement, reveals that they often discard informative background cues, disrupt contextual consistency, and lose fine-grained semantics, negatively affecting 3D detection. To overcome these limitations, we propose SEPatch3D, a novel framework that dynamically adjusts patch sizes while preserving critical semantic information within coarse patches. Specifically, we design Spatiotemporal-aware Patch Size Selection (SPSS) that assigns small patches to scenes containing nearby objects to preserve fine details and large patches to background-dominated scenes to reduce computation cost. To further mitigate potential detail loss, Informative Patch Selection (IPS) selects the informative patches for feature refinement, and Cross-Granularity Feature Enhancement (CGFE) injects fine-grained details into selected coarse patches, enriching semantic features. Experiments on the nuScenes and Argoverse 2 validation sets show that SEPatch3D achieves up to \textbf{57\%} faster inference than the StreamPETR baseline and \textbf{20\%} higher efficiency than the state-of-the-art ToC3D-faster, while preserving comparable detection accuracy. Code is available at https://github.com/Mingqj/SEPatch3D.

AI.tldr: SEPatch3D accelerates Vision Transformer-based 3D object detectors by dynamically adjusting patch sizes based on scene content, preserving fine details for nearby objects while using larger patches for background areas to reduce computation.
AI.motivation: Existing token compression methods for ViT-based 3D object detectors (pruning, merging, patch enlargement) often discard informative background cues, disrupt contextual consistency, and lose fine-grained semantics, negatively affecting detection accuracy while trying to reduce inference latency.
AI.method: Proposes SEPatch3D with three key components: 1) Spatiotemporal-aware Patch Size Selection (SPSS) that dynamically assigns small patches to scenes with nearby objects and large patches to background-dominated scenes; 2) Informative Patch Selection (IPS) that selects informative patches for feature refinement; 3) Cross-Granularity Feature Enhancement (CGFE) that injects fine-grained details into selected coarse patches.
AI.result: Achieves up to 57% faster inference than StreamPETR baseline and 20% higher efficiency than state-of-the-art ToC3D-faster, while preserving comparable detection accuracy on nuScenes and Argoverse 2 validation sets.
AI.conclusion: SEPatch3D effectively balances computation efficiency and detection accuracy by adaptively adjusting patch granularity based on scene content, overcoming limitations of existing token compression methods for multi-view 3D object detection.

title: Trajectory Planning for a Multi-UAV Rigid-Payload Cascaded Transportation System Based on Enhanced Tube-RRT*
comment: 15 pages, 7 figures. Under review at IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems (TAES). This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: This paper presents a two-stage trajectory planning framework for a multi-UAV rigid-payload cascaded transportation system, aiming to address planning challenges in densely cluttered environments. In Stage I, an Enhanced Tube-RRT* algorithm is developed by integrating active hybrid sampling and an adaptive expansion strategy, enabling rapid generation of a safe and feasible virtual tube in environments with dense obstacles. Moreover, a trajectory smoothness cost is explicitly incorporated into the edge cost to reduce excessive turns and thereby mitigate cable-induced oscillations. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed Enhanced Tube-RRT* achieves a higher success rate and effective sampling rate than mixed-sampling Tube-RRT* (STube-RRT*) and adaptive-extension Tube-RRT* (AETube-RRT*), while producing a shorter optimal path with a smaller cumulative turning angle. In Stage II, a convex quadratic program is formulated by considering payload translational and rotational dynamics, cable tension constraints, and collision-safety constraints, yielding a smooth, collision-free desired payload trajectory. Finally, a centralized geometric control scheme is applied to the cascaded system to validate the effectiveness and feasibility of the proposed planning framework, offering a practical solution for payload attitude maneuvering in densely cluttered environments.

AI.tldr: Two-stage planning framework for multi-UAV payload transport in cluttered environments: Stage I uses Enhanced Tube-RRT* for safe virtual tube generation, Stage II uses convex optimization for smooth payload trajectory, validated with geometric control.
AI.motivation: Address planning challenges for multi-UAV rigid-payload transportation systems in densely cluttered environments, where traditional methods struggle with obstacle avoidance, cable-induced oscillations, and complex dynamics.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: Stage I - Enhanced Tube-RRT* algorithm with active hybrid sampling and adaptive expansion for virtual tube generation; Stage II - convex quadratic program considering payload dynamics, cable tension, and collision constraints for trajectory optimization.
AI.result: Enhanced Tube-RRT* achieves higher success rate and effective sampling rate than STube-RRT* and AETube-RRT*, with shorter optimal path and smaller cumulative turning angle. The framework produces smooth, collision-free payload trajectories validated through centralized geometric control.
AI.conclusion: The proposed two-stage planning framework provides an effective solution for multi-UAV payload transportation in cluttered environments, successfully addressing cable-induced oscillations, collision avoidance, and complex dynamics through integrated sampling, optimization, and control approaches.

title: Geometric Metrics for MoE Specialization: From Fisher Information to Early Failure Detection
summary: Expert specialization is fundamental to Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model success, yet existing metrics (cosine similarity, routing entropy) lack theoretical grounding and yield inconsistent conclusions under reparameterization. We present an information-geometric framework providing the first rigorous characterization of MoE specialization dynamics. Our key insight is that expert routing distributions evolve on the probability simplex equipped with the Fisher information metric, enabling formal analysis via Riemannian geometry. We prove that standard heuristic metrics violate parameterization invariance (Theorem 1), establish that specialization corresponds to geodesic flow with quantified approximation bounds (Theorem 2), and derive a failure predictor with theoretical threshold justification (Theorem 3). The framework introduces two principled metrics: Fisher Specialization Index (FSI) achieving r=0.91+/-0.02 correlation with downstream performance, and Fisher Heterogeneity Score (FHS) predicting training failure at 10% completion with AUC=0.89+/-0.03 -- outperforming validation-loss-based early stopping by 23% while requiring 40x fewer compute cycles. We validate intervention protocols achieving 87% recovery rate when FHS>1 is detected. Comprehensive experiments across language modeling (WikiText-103, C4), vision MoE (ImageNet), and scaling studies (8-64 experts, 125M-2.7B parameters) validate our theoretical predictions.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an information-geometric framework for analyzing Mixture-of-Experts specialization, introducing theoretically-grounded metrics that outperform existing heuristic approaches and enable early failure prediction.
AI.motivation: Existing metrics for analyzing expert specialization in Mixture-of-Experts models (cosine similarity, routing entropy) lack theoretical grounding and yield inconsistent conclusions under reparameterization, creating a need for rigorous characterization of MoE specialization dynamics.
AI.method: Developed an information-geometric framework treating expert routing distributions as evolving on the probability simplex equipped with the Fisher information metric, enabling formal analysis via Riemannian geometry. Introduced two principled metrics: Fisher Specialization Index (FSI) and Fisher Heterogeneity Score (FHS).
AI.result: FSI achieves r=0.91+/-0.02 correlation with downstream performance; FHS predicts training failure at 10% completion with AUC=0.89+/-0.03, outperforming validation-loss-based early stopping by 23% while requiring 40x fewer compute cycles. Intervention protocols achieve 87% recovery rate when FHS>1 is detected.
AI.conclusion: The information-geometric framework provides the first rigorous characterization of MoE specialization dynamics, offering theoretically-grounded metrics that enable better understanding, monitoring, and intervention in MoE training, validated across diverse domains and scales.

title: Learning Adaptive Reasoning Paths for Efficient Visual Reasoning
summary: Visual reasoning models (VRMs) have recently shown strong cross-modal reasoning capabilities by integrating visual perception with language reasoning. However, they often suffer from overthinking, producing unnecessarily long reasoning chains for any tasks. We attribute this issue to \textbf{Reasoning Path Redundancy} in visual reasoning: many visual questions do not require the full reasoning process. To address this, we propose \textbf{AVR}, an adaptive visual reasoning framework that decomposes visual reasoning into three cognitive functions: visual perception, logical reasoning, and answer application. It further enables models to dynamically choose among three response formats: Full Format, Perception-Only Format, and Direct Answer. AVR is trained with FS-GRPO, an adaptation of Group Relative Policy Optimization that encourages the model to select the most efficient reasoning format while preserving correctness. Experiments on multiple vision-language benchmarks show that AVR reduces token usage by 50--90\% while maintaining overall accuracy, especially in perception-intensive tasks. These results demonstrate that adaptive visual reasoning can effectively mitigate overthinking in VRMs. Code and data are available at: https://github.com/RunRiotComeOn/AVR.

AI.tldr: AVR is an adaptive visual reasoning framework that reduces overthinking in VRMs by dynamically selecting among three response formats, cutting token usage by 50-90% while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Visual reasoning models suffer from overthinking, producing unnecessarily long reasoning chains for tasks that don't require full reasoning processes, due to Reasoning Path Redundancy.
AI.method: AVR decomposes visual reasoning into three cognitive functions (visual perception, logical reasoning, answer application) and enables dynamic selection among three response formats: Full Format, Perception-Only Format, and Direct Answer, trained with FS-GRPO adaptation of Group Relative Policy Optimization.
AI.result: AVR reduces token usage by 50-90% while maintaining overall accuracy across multiple vision-language benchmarks, especially effective in perception-intensive tasks.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive visual reasoning effectively mitigates overthinking in VRMs, demonstrating that many visual questions don't require full reasoning processes.

title: NEAT-NC: NEAT guided Navigation Cells for Robot Path Planning
comment: To appear in short form in Genetic and Evolutionary Computation Conference (GECCO '26), 2026
summary: To navigate a space, the brain makes an internal representation of the environment using different cells such as place cells, grid cells, head direction cells, border cells, and speed cells. All these cells, along with sensory inputs, enable an organism to explore the space around it. Inspired by these biological principles, we developed NEATNC, a Neuro-Evolution of Augmenting Topology guided Navigation Cells. The goal of the paper is to improve NEAT algorithm performance in path planning in dynamic environments using spatial cognitive cells. This approach uses navigation cells as inputs and evolves recurrent neural networks, representing the hippocampus part of the brain. The performance of the proposed algorithm is evaluated in different static and dynamic scenarios. This study highlights NEAT's adaptability to complex and different environments, showcasing the utility of biological theories. This suggests that our approach is well-suited for real-time dynamic path planning for robotics and games.

AI.tldr: NEATNC improves NEAT algorithm for dynamic path planning by incorporating biological navigation cells (place, grid, head direction, border, speed cells) as inputs to evolve recurrent neural networks representing hippocampus function.
AI.motivation: To enhance NEAT algorithm performance in dynamic path planning by drawing inspiration from biological spatial navigation systems in the brain, specifically the hippocampus and its specialized cells.
AI.method: Developed NEATNC (Neuro-Evolution of Augmenting Topology guided Navigation Cells) that uses biological navigation cells as inputs and evolves recurrent neural networks to represent hippocampal function for spatial navigation.
AI.result: The algorithm shows improved performance in both static and dynamic environments, demonstrating NEAT's adaptability to complex scenarios and highlighting the utility of biological theories in computational approaches.
AI.conclusion: The approach is well-suited for real-time dynamic path planning applications in robotics and games, successfully bridging biological navigation principles with evolutionary computation techniques.

title: Perspective on Bias in Biomedical AI: Preventing Downstream Healthcare Disparities
summary: Healthcare disparities persist across socioeconomic boundaries, often attributed to unequal access to screening, diagnostics, and therapeutics. However, this perspective highlights that critical biases can emerge much earlier, during data collection and research prioritization, long before clinical implementation in cases where the focus of the studies and the data that is collected is at the molecular level. A vast number of studies focus on collecting omics data but the demographic information associated with these datasets is often not reported in the studies, and when it is reported, it shows big biases. An automated analysis of 4719 PubMed-indexed omics publications from 2015 to 2024 reveals that only a small fraction report ancestry or ethnicity information, with ancestry reporting improving slightly. Analysis of large-scale datasets commonly used for model training, such as CellxGene and GEO, reveals substantial population bias where European-ancestry data dominates. As biomedical foundation models become central to biomedical discovery with a paradigm in which base models are pretrained on large datasets and reusing them time and again for many different downstream tasks, they risk perpetuating or amplifying these early-stage biases, leading to cascading inequities that regulatory interventions cannot fully reverse. We propose a community-wide focus on three foundational principles: Provenance, Openness, and Evaluation Transparency to improve equity and robustness in biomedical AI. This approach aims to foster biomedical innovation that more effectively serves underserved populations and improves health outcomes.

AI.tldr: Analysis of 4719 omics studies shows severe underreporting of demographic data and European ancestry bias in biomedical datasets, risking amplification by foundation models; proposes Provenance, Openness, Evaluation Transparency principles.
AI.motivation: Healthcare disparities persist due to biases that emerge early in data collection and research prioritization, particularly in omics studies where demographic information is often missing or biased, risking perpetuation by biomedical foundation models.
AI.method: Automated analysis of 4719 PubMed-indexed omics publications (2015-2024) for ancestry/ethnicity reporting, plus examination of large-scale datasets (CellxGene, GEO) for population representation biases.
AI.result: Only small fraction of omics studies report ancestry/ethnicity data; ancestry reporting improved slightly over time; European-ancestry data dominates major datasets, creating substantial population bias.
AI.conclusion: Biomedical foundation models risk amplifying early-stage biases; proposes community adoption of Provenance, Openness, and Evaluation Transparency principles to improve equity and robustness in biomedical AI.

title: Deepfake Detection Generalization with Diffusion Noise
summary: Deepfake detectors face growing challenges in generalization as new image synthesis techniques emerge. In particular, deepfakes generated by diffusion models are highly photorealistic and often evade detectors trained on GAN-based forgeries. This paper addresses the generalization problem in deepfake detection by leveraging diffusion noise characteristics. We propose an Attention-guided Noise Learning (ANL) framework that integrates a pre-trained diffusion model into the deepfake detection pipeline to guide the learning of more robust features. Specifically, our method uses the diffusion model's denoising process to expose subtle artifacts: the detector is trained to predict the noise contained in an input image at a given diffusion step, forcing it to capture discrepancies between real and synthetic images, while an attention-guided mechanism derived from the predicted noise is introduced to encourage the model to focus on globally distributed discrepancies rather than local patterns. By harnessing the frozen diffusion model's learned distribution of natural images, the ANL method acts as a form of regularization, improving the detector's generalization to unseen forgery types. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ANL significantly outperforms existing methods on multiple benchmarks, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy in detecting diffusion-generated deepfakes. Notably, the proposed framework boosts generalization performance (e.g., improving ACC/AP by a substantial margin on unseen models) without introducing additional overhead during inference. Our results highlight that diffusion noise provides a powerful signal for generalizable deepfake detection.

AI.tldr: ANL framework uses diffusion model noise prediction to improve deepfake detector generalization, achieving SOTA on diffusion-generated forgeries without inference overhead.
AI.motivation: Deepfake detectors struggle to generalize to new synthesis techniques, especially diffusion models that evade GAN-trained detectors. Need robust detection that works across unseen forgery types.
AI.method: Attention-guided Noise Learning (ANL) integrates frozen diffusion model into detection pipeline. Detector learns to predict noise at given diffusion step, capturing discrepancies between real/synthetic images. Attention mechanism from predicted noise focuses on globally distributed artifacts rather than local patterns.
AI.result: Significantly outperforms existing methods on multiple benchmarks, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy for diffusion-generated deepfakes. Substantially improves ACC/AP on unseen models without inference overhead.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion noise provides powerful signal for generalizable deepfake detection. ANL framework effectively leverages diffusion model's natural image distribution as regularization to improve detector generalization to unseen forgery types.

title: Dual Pose-Graph Semantic Localization for Vision-Based Autonomous Drone Racing
summary: Autonomous drone racing demands robust real-time localization under extreme conditions: high-speed flight, aggressive maneuvers, and payload-constrained platforms that often rely on a single camera for perception. Existing visual SLAM systems, while effective in general scenarios, struggle with motion blur and feature instability inherent to racing dynamics, and do not exploit the structured nature of racing environments. In this work, we present a dual pose-graph architecture that fuses odometry with semantic detections for robust localization. A temporary graph accumulates multiple gate observations between keyframes and optimizes them into a single refined constraint per landmark, which is then promoted to a persistent main graph. This design preserves the information richness of frequent detections while preventing graph growth from degrading real-time performance. The system is designed to be sensor-agnostic, although in this work we validate it using monocular visual-inertial odometry and visual gate detections. Experimental evaluation on the TII-RATM dataset shows a 56% to 74% reduction in ATE compared to standalone VIO, while an ablation study confirms that the dual-graph architecture achieves 10% to 12% higher accuracy than a single-graph baseline at identical computational cost. Deployment in the A2RL competition demonstrated that the system performs real-time onboard localization during flight, reducing the drift of the odometry baseline by up to 4.2 m per lap.

AI.tldr: Dual pose-graph SLAM system for drone racing that fuses odometry with semantic gate detections, reducing drift by 56-74% compared to VIO alone while maintaining real-time performance.
AI.motivation: Autonomous drone racing requires robust real-time localization under extreme conditions (high-speed, aggressive maneuvers) where existing visual SLAM systems struggle with motion blur and feature instability, and don't exploit the structured racing environment.
AI.method: Dual pose-graph architecture: temporary graph accumulates multiple gate observations between keyframes and optimizes them into single refined constraints, then promotes to persistent main graph. Sensor-agnostic design validated with monocular visual-inertial odometry and visual gate detections.
AI.result: 56-74% reduction in ATE compared to standalone VIO on TII-RATM dataset; dual-graph achieves 10-12% higher accuracy than single-graph baseline at same computational cost; reduces drift by up to 4.2m per lap in A2RL competition.
AI.conclusion: The dual pose-graph architecture effectively fuses odometry with semantic detections for robust drone racing localization, preserving information richness while maintaining real-time performance, demonstrating practical deployment in competitive racing scenarios.

title: Mind DeepResearch Technical Report
summary: We present \textbf{Mind DeepResearch (MindDR)}, an efficient multi-agent deep research framework that achieves leading performance with only \textasciitilde30B-parameter models through a meticulously designed data synthesis and multi-stage training pipeline. The core innovation of MindDR lies in a collaborative three-agent architecture (Planning Agent, DeepSearch Agent, and Report Agent) and a four-stage agent-specialized training pipeline comprising SFT cold-start, Search-RL, Report-RL and preference alignment. With this regime, MindDR demonstrates competitive performance even with \textasciitilde30B-scale models. Specifically, MindDR achieves 45.7\% on BrowseComp-ZH, 42.8\% on BrowseComp, 46.5\% on WideSearch, 75.0\% on xbench-DS, and 52.5 on DeepResearch Bench, outperforming comparable-scale open-source agent systems and rivaling larger-scale models. MindDR has been deployed as an online product in Li Auto. Furthermore, we introduce \textbf{MindDR Bench}, a curated benchmark of 500 real-world Chinese queries from our internal product user interactions, evaluated through a comprehensive multi-dimensional rubric system rather than relying on a single RACE metric. On MindDR Bench, MindDR achieves a state-of-the-art score of 51.8.

AI.tldr: MindDR is a multi-agent deep research framework using ~30B-parameter models that achieves competitive performance through specialized agent training and data synthesis, deployed in Li Auto with new benchmark.
AI.motivation: To create an efficient deep research system that can achieve leading performance with smaller-scale models (~30B parameters) rather than relying on massive models, making it practical for real-world deployment.
AI.method: Three-agent architecture (Planning, DeepSearch, Report Agents) with four-stage training: SFT cold-start, Search-RL, Report-RL, and preference alignment, plus data synthesis pipeline.
AI.result: Achieves 45.7% on BrowseComp-ZH, 42.8% on BrowseComp, 46.5% on WideSearch, 75.0% on xbench-DS, 52.5 on DeepResearch Bench, outperforming comparable open-source systems and rivaling larger models.
AI.conclusion: MindDR demonstrates that carefully designed multi-agent architectures and specialized training pipelines can achieve competitive deep research performance with efficient ~30B-parameter models, enabling practical deployment in real products like Li Auto.

title: M3D-Net: Multi-Modal 3D Facial Feature Reconstruction Network for Deepfake Detection
summary: With the rapid advancement of deep learning in image generation, facial forgery techniques have achieved unprecedented realism, posing serious threats to cybersecurity and information authenticity. Most existing deepfake detection approaches rely on the reconstruction of isolated facial attributes without fully exploiting the complementary nature of multi-modal feature representations. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a novel Multi-Modal 3D Facial Feature Reconstruction Network (M3D-Net) for deepfake detection. Our method leverages an end-to-end dual-stream architecture that reconstructs fine-grained facial geometry and reflectance properties from single-view RGB images via a self-supervised 3D facial reconstruction module. The network further enhances detection performance through a 3D Feature Pre-fusion Module (PFM), which adaptively adjusts multi-scale features, and a Multi-modal Fusion Module (MFM) that effectively integrates RGB and 3D-reconstructed features using attention mechanisms. Extensive experiments on multiple public datasets demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance in terms of detection accuracy and robustness, significantly outperforming existing methods while exhibiting strong generalization across diverse scenarios.

AI.tldr: M3D-Net: A multi-modal 3D facial feature reconstruction network for deepfake detection that outperforms existing methods through 3D facial reconstruction and attention-based feature fusion.
AI.motivation: Deepfake techniques have become highly realistic, threatening cybersecurity and information authenticity. Existing detection methods often fail to fully exploit complementary multi-modal features and rely on isolated facial attribute reconstruction.
AI.method: Proposes M3D-Net with dual-stream architecture: 1) self-supervised 3D facial reconstruction module for geometry and reflectance properties from RGB images, 2) 3D Feature Pre-fusion Module for multi-scale feature adjustment, 3) Multi-modal Fusion Module using attention mechanisms to integrate RGB and 3D features.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple public datasets with superior detection accuracy, robustness, and strong generalization across diverse scenarios, significantly outperforming existing methods.
AI.conclusion: M3D-Net effectively addresses deepfake detection challenges by leveraging multi-modal 3D facial feature reconstruction and attention-based fusion, providing a robust solution for cybersecurity applications.

title: Benchmarking Classical Coverage Path Planning Heuristics on Irregular Hexagonal Grids for Maritime Coverage Scenarios
summary: Coverage path planning on irregular hexagonal grids is relevant to maritime surveillance, search and rescue and environmental monitoring, yet classical methods are often compared on small ad hoc examples or on rectangular grids. This paper presents a reproducible benchmark of deterministic single-vehicle coverage path planning heuristics on irregular hexagonal graphs derived from synthetic but maritime-motivated areas of interest. The benchmark contains 10,000 Hamiltonian-feasible instances spanning compact, elongated, and irregular morphologies, 17 heuristics from seven families, and a common evaluation protocol covering Hamiltonian success, complete-coverage success, revisits, path length, heading changes, and CPU latency. Across the released dataset, heuristics with explicit shortest-path reconnection solve the relaxed coverage task reliably but almost never produce zero-revisit tours. Exact Depth-First Search confirms that every released instance is Hamiltonian-feasible. The strongest classical Hamiltonian baseline is a Warnsdorff variant that uses an index-based tie-break together with a terminal-inclusive residual-degree policy, reaching 79.0% Hamiltonian success. The dominant design choice is not tie-breaking alone, but how the residual degree is defined when the endpoint is reserved until the final move. This shows that underreported implementation details can materially affect performance on sparse geometric graphs with bottlenecks. The benchmark is intended as a controlled testbed for heuristic analysis rather than as a claim of operational optimality at fleet scale.

AI.tldr: Benchmark of 17 coverage path planning heuristics on 10,000 irregular hexagonal graphs shows that explicit shortest-path reconnection solves relaxed coverage but rarely produces zero-revisit tours, with best Hamiltonian success at 79.0% using a Warnsdorff variant with specific tie-breaking and residual-degree policy.
AI.motivation: Coverage path planning on irregular hexagonal grids is important for maritime surveillance, search and rescue, and environmental monitoring, but existing methods lack standardized comparison on realistic irregular geometries rather than just rectangular grids.
AI.method: Created reproducible benchmark with 10,000 Hamiltonian-feasible instances on irregular hexagonal graphs with maritime-motivated morphologies, evaluated 17 heuristics from 7 families using metrics including Hamiltonian success, coverage success, revisits, path length, heading changes, and CPU latency.
AI.result: Heuristics with explicit shortest-path reconnection reliably solve relaxed coverage but almost never produce zero-revisit tours; best Hamiltonian success is 79.0% using Warnsdorff variant with index-based tie-break and terminal-inclusive residual-degree policy; implementation details significantly affect performance on sparse geometric graphs with bottlenecks.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark provides controlled testbed for heuristic analysis, showing that underreported implementation details materially affect performance on sparse geometric graphs, with residual-degree definition being the dominant design choice when endpoint is reserved until final move.

title: Quantifying Cross-Query Contradictions in Multi-Query LLM Reasoning
comment: Accepted at the ICLR 2026 Workshop on Logical Reasoning of Large Language Models. 9 pages, 6 tables, code and data at https://huggingface.co/datasets/rohitspider/cross_query_benchmark
summary: Large language models frequently produce mutually inconsistent answers when reasoning over multiple related queries. We study case-file logical consistency: maintaining a globally satisfiable belief state across interdependent queries. We introduce a benchmark of 390 multi-query reasoning instances with entailment/contradiction/unknown labels and propose set-level metrics including Case Satisfiability Rate, Contradiction Density and Revision Cost. Our solver-augmented approach extracts commitments, verifies global satisfiability and performs counterexample-guided repair. Across four reasoning domains, our method substantially reduces cross-query contradictions (SetCons: 0.56 to 0.94) while preserving per-query accuracy, demonstrating that global coherence is critical for robust multi-query reasoning.

AI.tldr: A method for improving logical consistency across multiple related queries in LLMs using solver-augmented verification and repair.
AI.motivation: LLMs often produce inconsistent answers when reasoning over multiple related queries, which undermines reliability in multi-query reasoning scenarios.
AI.method: Solver-augmented approach that extracts commitments from LLM responses, verifies global satisfiability, and performs counterexample-guided repair to resolve contradictions.
AI.result: Method reduces cross-query contradictions significantly (SetCons: 0.56 to 0.94) while maintaining per-query accuracy across four reasoning domains.
AI.conclusion: Global coherence is critical for robust multi-query reasoning, and the proposed approach effectively maintains logical consistency across interdependent queries.

title: TurboTalk: Progressive Distillation for One-Step Audio-Driven Talking Avatar Generation
summary: Existing audio-driven video digital human generation models rely on multi-step denoising, resulting in substantial computational overhead that severely limits their deployment in real-world settings. While one-step distillation approaches can significantly accelerate inference, they often suffer from training instability. To address this challenge, we propose TurboTalk, a two-stage progressive distillation framework that effectively compresses a multi-step audio-driven video diffusion model into a single-step generator. We first adopt Distribution Matching Distillation to obtain a strong and stable 4-step student, and then progressively reduce the denoising steps from 4 to 1 through adversarial distillation. To ensure stable training under extreme step reduction, we introduce a progressive timestep sampling strategy and a self-compare adversarial objective that provides an intermediate adversarial reference that stabilizes progressive distillation. Our method achieve single-step generation of video talking avatar, boosting inference speed by 120 times while maintaining high generation quality.

AI.tldr: TurboTalk is a progressive distillation framework that compresses multi-step audio-driven video diffusion models into single-step generators, achieving 120x speedup while maintaining quality.
AI.motivation: Existing audio-driven video generation models require multi-step denoising which causes high computational overhead, limiting real-world deployment. One-step distillation approaches exist but suffer from training instability.
AI.method: Two-stage progressive distillation: 1) Distribution Matching Distillation to get stable 4-step student, 2) Progressive reduction from 4 to 1 step via adversarial distillation with progressive timestep sampling and self-compare adversarial objective for stability.
AI.result: Achieves single-step generation of video talking avatars with 120x inference speed boost while maintaining high generation quality.
AI.conclusion: TurboTalk effectively addresses training instability in extreme step reduction for audio-driven video generation, enabling efficient single-step inference suitable for real-world deployment.

title: A Hierarchical Spatiotemporal Action Tokenizer for In-Context Imitation Learning in Robotics
summary: We present a novel hierarchical spatiotemporal action tokenizer for in-context imitation learning. We first propose a hierarchical approach, which consists of two successive levels of vector quantization. In particular, the lower level assigns input actions to fine-grained subclusters, while the higher level further maps fine-grained subclusters to clusters. Our hierarchical approach outperforms the non-hierarchical counterpart, while mainly exploiting spatial information by reconstructing input actions. Furthermore, we extend our approach by utilizing both spatial and temporal cues, forming a hierarchical spatiotemporal action tokenizer, namely HiST-AT. Specifically, our hierarchical spatiotemporal approach conducts multi-level clustering, while simultaneously recovering input actions and their associated timestamps. Finally, extensive evaluations on multiple simulation and real robotic manipulation benchmarks show that our approach establishes a new state-of-the-art performance in in-context imitation learning.

AI.tldr: HiST-AT: Hierarchical spatiotemporal action tokenizer for in-context imitation learning using multi-level vector quantization with spatial and temporal cues
AI.motivation: To improve in-context imitation learning by developing a more effective action representation method that captures both spatial and temporal information in a hierarchical manner
AI.method: Two-level hierarchical vector quantization: lower level for fine-grained subclusters, higher level for clusters. Extended to HiST-AT with spatiotemporal cues that simultaneously recovers actions and timestamps through multi-level clustering
AI.result: Outperforms non-hierarchical counterparts, establishes new state-of-the-art performance on multiple simulation and real robotic manipulation benchmarks
AI.conclusion: HiST-AT is an effective hierarchical spatiotemporal action tokenizer that significantly advances in-context imitation learning capabilities

title: Dissecting Failure Dynamics in Large Language Model Reasoning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve strong performance through extended inference-time deliberation, yet how their reasoning failures arise remains poorly understood. By analyzing model-generated reasoning trajectories, we find that errors are not uniformly distributed but often originate from a small number of early transition points, after which reasoning remains locally coherent but globally incorrect. These transitions coincide with localized spikes in token-level entropy, and alternative continuations from the same intermediate state can still lead to correct solutions. Based on these observations, we introduce GUARD, a targeted inference-time framework that probes and redirects critical transitions using uncertainty signals. Empirical evaluations across multiple benchmarks confirm that interventions guided by these failure dynamics lead to more reliable reasoning outcomes. Our findings highlight the importance of understanding when and how reasoning first deviates, complementing existing approaches that focus on scaling inference-time computation.

AI.tldr: GUARD: A targeted inference-time framework that identifies and redirects critical early reasoning transitions where LLMs first deviate from correct solutions, using uncertainty signals to improve reasoning reliability.
AI.motivation: While LLMs achieve strong performance through extended inference-time deliberation, how their reasoning failures arise remains poorly understood. The paper aims to understand when and why reasoning first deviates from correct solutions, complementing existing approaches that focus on scaling inference-time computation.
AI.method: Analyze model-generated reasoning trajectories to identify that errors originate from small number of early transition points with localized token-level entropy spikes. Introduce GUARD framework that probes and redirects these critical transitions using uncertainty signals during inference.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations across multiple benchmarks confirm that interventions guided by these failure dynamics lead to more reliable reasoning outcomes. The approach effectively identifies critical transition points where alternative continuations from the same intermediate state can still lead to correct solutions.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning errors in LLMs are not uniformly distributed but originate from specific early transition points. Understanding when and how reasoning first deviates is crucial, and targeted interventions at these critical transitions can significantly improve reasoning reliability without simply scaling computation.

title: MapSR: Prompt-Driven Land Cover Map Super-Resolution via Vision Foundation Models
summary: High-resolution (HR) land-cover mapping is often constrained by the high cost of dense HR annotations. We revisit this problem from the perspective of map super-resolution, which enhances coarse low-resolution (LR) land-cover products into HR maps at the resolution of the input imagery. Existing weakly supervised methods can leverage LR labels, but they typically use them to retrain dense predictors with substantial computational cost. We propose MapSR, a prompt-driven framework that decouples supervision from model training. MapSR uses LR labels once to extract class prompts from frozen vision foundation model features through a lightweight linear probe, after which HR mapping proceeds via training-free metric inference and graph-based prediction refinement. Specifically, class prompts are estimated by aggregating high-confidence HR features identified by the linear probe, and HR predictions are obtained by cosine-similarity matching followed by graph-based propagation for spatial refinement. Experiments on the Chesapeake Bay dataset show that MapSR achieves 59.64% mIoU without any HR labels, remaining competitive with the strongest weakly supervised baseline and surpassing a fully supervised baseline. Notably, MapSR reduces trainable parameters by four orders of magnitude and shortens training time from hours to minutes, enabling scalable HR mapping under limited annotation and compute budgets. The code is available at https://github.com/rikirikirikiriki/MapSR.

AI.tldr: MapSR is a prompt-driven framework for high-resolution land-cover mapping that enhances coarse low-resolution labels into HR maps without HR supervision, using frozen foundation models and training-free inference.
AI.motivation: High-resolution land-cover mapping is expensive due to dense HR annotation costs. Existing weakly supervised methods require retraining dense predictors with substantial computational resources.
AI.method: MapSR decouples supervision from training: uses LR labels once to extract class prompts from frozen vision foundation model features via lightweight linear probe, then performs training-free metric inference (cosine-similarity matching) and graph-based prediction refinement.
AI.result: Achieves 59.64% mIoU on Chesapeake Bay dataset without HR labels, competitive with strongest weakly supervised baseline, surpasses fully supervised baseline. Reduces trainable parameters by 4 orders of magnitude and training time from hours to minutes.
AI.conclusion: MapSR enables scalable high-resolution land-cover mapping under limited annotation and compute budgets by leveraging frozen foundation models and eliminating expensive retraining.

title: Vision-Based Safe Human-Robot Collaboration with Uncertainty Guarantees
summary: We propose a framework for vision-based human pose estimation and motion prediction that gives conformal prediction guarantees for certifiably safe human-robot collaboration. Our framework combines aleatoric uncertainty estimation with OOD detection for high probabilistic confidence. To integrate our pipeline in certifiable safety frameworks, we propose conformal prediction sets for human motion predictions with high, valid confidence. We evaluate our pipeline on recorded human motion data and a real-world human-robot collaboration setting.

AI.tldr: Vision-based human pose estimation with conformal prediction guarantees for safe human-robot collaboration
AI.motivation: Need for certifiably safe human-robot collaboration with probabilistic confidence guarantees in vision-based pose estimation
AI.method: Combines aleatoric uncertainty estimation with OOD detection, proposes conformal prediction sets for human motion predictions
AI.result: Framework provides high, valid confidence guarantees for human motion predictions in collaborative settings
AI.conclusion: Proposed framework enables certifiably safe human-robot collaboration through conformal prediction guarantees

title: TRACER: Trace-Based Adaptive Cost-Efficient Routing for LLM Classification
summary:
Every call to an LLM classification endpoint produces a labeled input-output pair already retained in production logs. These pairs constitute a free, growing training set: a lightweight surrogate trained on them can absorb a significant portion of future traffic at near-zero marginal inference cost. The open questions are when the surrogate is reliable enough to deploy, what it handles versus defers, and how that boundary evolves as data accumulates.
  We introduce TRACER (Trace-based Adaptive Cost-Efficient Routing), an open-source system that trains ML surrogates on an LLM's own production traces and governs deployment through a parity gate: the surrogate is activated only when its agreement with the LLM exceeds a user-specified threshold α. To make the routing boundary transparent, TRACER generates interpretability artifacts describing which input regions the surrogate handles, where it plateaus, and why it defers.
  On a 77-class intent benchmark with a Sonnet 4.6 teacher, TRACER achieves 83-100% surrogate coverage depending on the quality target α; on a 150-class benchmark, the surrogate fully replaces the teacher. On a natural language inference task, the parity gate correctly refuses deployment because the embedding representation cannot support reliable separation. The system is available as open-source software.

AI.tldr: TRACER trains lightweight ML surrogates on LLM production logs to handle classification traffic at near-zero cost, using a parity gate to ensure reliability and providing interpretability for routing decisions.
AI.motivation: LLM classification endpoints generate free training data in production logs that could be used to create efficient surrogates, but questions remain about when to deploy them, what they should handle vs defer, and how their capabilities evolve with more data.
AI.method: TRACER trains ML surrogates on LLM production traces and uses a parity gate that only activates the surrogate when its agreement with the LLM exceeds a user-specified threshold α. It generates interpretability artifacts to explain which input regions the surrogate handles, where it plateaus, and why it defers.
AI.result: On a 77-class intent benchmark with Sonnet 4.6 teacher, TRACER achieves 83-100% surrogate coverage depending on α; on a 150-class benchmark, the surrogate fully replaces the teacher. On natural language inference, the parity gate correctly refuses deployment when embeddings can't support reliable separation.
AI.conclusion: TRACER provides a practical system for cost-efficient LLM traffic routing using production data, with transparent deployment decisions and interpretability for routing boundaries, available as open-source software.

title: Prompt-Guided Image Editing with Masked Logit Nudging in Visual Autoregressive Models
comment: Accepted at the 2026 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition Findings (CVPRF)
summary: We address the problem of prompt-guided image editing in visual autoregressive models. Given a source image and a target text prompt, we aim to modify the source image according to the target prompt, while preserving all regions which are unrelated to the requested edit. To this end, we present Masked Logit Nudging, which uses the source image token maps to introduce a guidance step that aligns the model's predictions under the target prompt with these source token maps. Specifically, we convert the fixed source encodings into logits using the VAR encoding, nudging the model's predicted logits towards the targets along a semantic trajectory defined by the source-target prompts. Edits are applied only within spatial masks obtained through a dedicated masking scheme that leverages cross-attention differences between the source and edited prompts. Then, we introduce a refinement to correct quantization errors and improve reconstruction quality. Our approach achieves the best image editing performance on the PIE benchmark at 512px and 1024px resolutions. Beyond editing, our method delivers faithful reconstructions and outperforms previous methods on COCO at 512px and OpenImages at 1024px. Overall, our method outperforms VAR-related approaches and achieves comparable or even better performance than diffusion models, while being much faster. Code is available at 'https://github.com/AmirMaEl/MLN'.

AI.tldr: Masked Logit Nudging enables prompt-guided image editing in visual autoregressive models by aligning predictions with source token maps and using cross-attention masking, achieving state-of-the-art performance while being faster than diffusion models.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of prompt-guided image editing in visual autoregressive models, where the goal is to modify source images according to target text prompts while preserving unrelated regions, which existing methods struggle with.
AI.method: Proposes Masked Logit Nudging that uses source image token maps to guide model predictions under target prompts, converts fixed source encodings into logits using VAR encoding, applies edits only within spatial masks obtained through cross-attention differences, and includes refinement to correct quantization errors.
AI.result: Achieves best image editing performance on PIE benchmark at 512px and 1024px resolutions, delivers faithful reconstructions, outperforms previous methods on COCO at 512px and OpenImages at 1024px, and outperforms VAR-related approaches while being comparable or better than diffusion models with much faster inference.
AI.conclusion: Masked Logit Nudging provides an effective solution for prompt-guided image editing in visual autoregressive models, achieving state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks while maintaining efficiency advantages over diffusion-based approaches.

title: Abstract Sim2Real through Approximate Information States
summary: In recent years, reinforcement learning (RL) has shown remarkable success in robotics when a fast and accurate simulator is available for a given task. When using RL and simulation, more simulator realism is generally beneficial but becomes harder to obtain as robots are deployed in increasingly complex and widescale domains. In such settings, simulators will likely fail to model all relevant details of a given target task and this observation motivates the study of sim2real with simulators that leave out key task details. In this paper, we formalize and study the abstract sim2real problem: given an abstract simulator that models a target task at a coarse level of abstraction, how can we train a policy with RL in the abstract simulator and successfully transfer it to the real-world? Our first contribution is to formalize this problem using the language of state abstraction from the RL literature. This framing shows that an abstract simulator can be grounded to match the target task if the grounded abstract dynamics take the history of states into account. Based on the formalism, we then introduce a method that uses real-world task data to correct the dynamics of the abstract simulator. We then show that this method enables successful policy transfer both in sim2sim and sim2real evaluation.

AI.tldr: This paper formalizes the abstract sim2real problem where simulators leave out key task details, and proposes a method using real-world data to correct abstract simulator dynamics for successful policy transfer.
AI.motivation: As robots are deployed in increasingly complex domains, simulators will likely fail to model all relevant details of target tasks, motivating the study of sim2real with abstract simulators that leave out key task details.
AI.method: The paper formalizes the abstract sim2real problem using state abstraction from RL literature, showing that abstract simulators can be grounded using history of states. It then introduces a method that uses real-world task data to correct the dynamics of the abstract simulator.
AI.result: The proposed method enables successful policy transfer both in sim2sim and sim2real evaluations, demonstrating effective transfer from abstract simulators to real-world tasks.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a formal framework for abstract sim2real problems and shows that correcting abstract simulator dynamics using real-world data enables successful policy transfer, addressing the challenge of incomplete simulator realism in complex domains.

title: MARS$^2$: Scaling Multi-Agent Tree Search via Reinforcement Learning for Code Generation
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) paradigms have demonstrated strong performance on reasoning-intensive tasks such as code generation. However, limited trajectory diversity often leads to diminishing returns, which constrains the achievable performance ceiling. Search-enhanced RL alleviates this issue by introducing structured exploration, which remains constrained by the single-agent policy priors. Meanwhile, leveraging multiple interacting policies can acquire more diverse exploratory signals, but existing approaches are typically decoupled from structured search. We propose \textbf{MARS$^2$} (Multi-Agent Reinforced Tree-Search Scaling), a unified RL framework in which multiple independently-optimized agents collaborate within a shared tree-structured search environment. MARS$^2$ models the search tree as a learnable multi-agent interaction environment, enabling heterogeneous agents to collaboratively generate and refine candidate solutions within a shared search topology. To support effective learning, we introduce a path-level group advantage formulation based on tree-consistent reward shaping, which facilitates effective credit assignment across complex search trajectories. Experiments on code generation benchmarks show that MARS$^2$ consistently improves performance across diverse model combinations and training settings, demonstrating the effectiveness of coupling multi-agent collaboration with tree search for enhancing reinforcement learning. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/TsinghuaC3I/MARTI.

AI.tldr: MARS² is a multi-agent RL framework that combines tree search with collaborative agents for better exploration in code generation tasks.
AI.motivation: Current RL approaches for reasoning tasks like code generation suffer from limited trajectory diversity and diminishing returns. Single-agent search methods are constrained by policy priors, while multi-agent approaches lack structured search integration.
AI.method: MARS² creates a unified RL framework where multiple independently-optimized agents collaborate within a shared tree-structured search environment. It models the search tree as a learnable multi-agent interaction environment and introduces path-level group advantage formulation with tree-consistent reward shaping for effective credit assignment.
AI.result: Experiments on code generation benchmarks show MARS² consistently improves performance across diverse model combinations and training settings, demonstrating the effectiveness of coupling multi-agent collaboration with tree search.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully enhances reinforcement learning by integrating multi-agent collaboration with structured tree search, overcoming limitations of both single-agent search and decoupled multi-agent approaches.

title: Towards Design Compositing
comment: Accepted at CVPR 2026 Workshop on CVEU
summary: Graphic design creation involves harmoniously assembling multimodal components such as images, text, logos, and other visual assets collected from diverse sources, into a visually-appealing and cohesive design. Recent methods have largely focused on layout prediction or complementary element generation, while retaining input elements exactly, implicitly assuming that provided components are already stylistically harmonious. In practice, inputs often come from disparate sources and exhibit visual mismatch, making this assumption limiting. We argue that identity-preserving stylization and compositing of input elements is a critical missing ingredient for truly harmonized components-to-design pipelines. To this end, we propose GIST, a training-free, identity-preserving image compositor that sits between layout prediction and typography generation, and can be plugged into any existing components-to-design or design-refining pipeline without modification. We demonstrate this by integrating GIST with two substantially different existing methods, LaDeCo and Design-o-meter. GIST shows significant improvements in visual harmony and aesthetic quality across both pipelines, as validated by LLaVA-OV and GPT-4V on aspect-wise ratings and pairwise preference over naive pasting. Project Page: abhinav-mahajan10.github.io/GIST/.

AI.tldr: GIST is a training-free, identity-preserving image compositor that harmonizes mismatched visual elements in graphic design pipelines, improving visual harmony without modifying existing components.
AI.motivation: Current graphic design methods assume input elements are already stylistically harmonious, but in practice they often come from disparate sources with visual mismatches. There's a need for identity-preserving stylization to create truly cohesive designs.
AI.method: GIST is a training-free, identity-preserving image compositor that sits between layout prediction and typography generation. It can be plugged into any existing components-to-design or design-refining pipeline without modification.
AI.result: When integrated with LaDeCo and Design-o-meter pipelines, GIST shows significant improvements in visual harmony and aesthetic quality, validated by LLaVA-OV and GPT-4V evaluations with pairwise preference over naive pasting.
AI.conclusion: GIST addresses the critical missing ingredient of identity-preserving stylization in components-to-design pipelines, enabling harmonization of mismatched visual elements while preserving their identity.

title: Efficient closed-form approaches for pose estimation using Sylvester forms
summary: Solving non-linear least-squares problem for pose estimation (rotation and translation) is often a time consuming yet fundamental problem in several real-time computer vision applications. With an adequate rotation parametrization, the optimization problem can be reduced to the solution of a~system of polynomial equations and solved in closed form. Recent advances in efficient closed form solvers utilizing resultant matrices have shown a promising research direction to decrease the computation time while preserving the estimation accuracy. In this paper, we propose a new class of resultant-based solvers that exploit Sylvester forms to further reduce the complexity of the resolution. We demonstrate that our proposed methods are numerically as accurate as the state-of-the-art solvers, and outperform them in terms of computational time. We show that this approach can be applied for pose estimation in two different types of problems: estimating a pose from 3D to 3D correspondences, and estimating a pose from 3D points to 2D points correspondences.

AI.tldr: New resultant-based solvers using Sylvester forms for pose estimation that are numerically accurate as state-of-the-art but faster computationally.
AI.motivation: Non-linear least-squares pose estimation is computationally expensive but fundamental for real-time computer vision applications. Recent resultant-based solvers show promise for reducing computation time while maintaining accuracy.
AI.method: Proposed a new class of resultant-based solvers that exploit Sylvester forms to reduce complexity. Applied to two pose estimation problems: 3D-to-3D correspondences and 3D-to-2D point correspondences.
AI.result: The proposed methods are numerically as accurate as state-of-the-art solvers and outperform them in terms of computational time.
AI.conclusion: Sylvester form-based resultant solvers provide an effective approach for faster pose estimation while maintaining accuracy, applicable to both 3D-3D and 3D-2D correspondence problems.

title: Enhancing Mental Health Counseling Support in Bangladesh using Culturally-Grounded Knowledge
summary: Large language models (LLMs) show promise in generating supportive responses for mental health and counseling applications. However, their responses often lack cultural sensitivity, contextual grounding, and clinically appropriate guidance. This work addresses the gap of how to systematically incorporate domain-specific, clinically validated knowledge into LLMs to improve counseling quality. We utilize and compare two approaches, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and a knowledge graph (KG)-based method, designed to support para-counselors. Our KG is constructed manually and clinically validated, capturing causal relationships between stressors, interventions, and outcomes, with contributions from multidisciplinary people. We evaluated multiple LLMs in both settings using BERTScore F1 and SBERT cosine similarity, as well as human evaluation across five metrics, which is designed to directly measure the effectiveness of counseling beyond similarity at the surface level. The results show that KG-based approaches consistently improve contextual relevance, clinical appropriateness, and practical usability compared to RAG alone, demonstrating that structured, expert-validated knowledge plays a critical role in addressing LLMs limitations in counseling tasks.

AI.tldr: KG-based LLM approach outperforms RAG for counseling by incorporating clinically validated knowledge graphs, improving cultural sensitivity and clinical appropriateness.
AI.motivation: LLMs show promise for mental health counseling but lack cultural sensitivity, contextual grounding, and clinically appropriate guidance, creating a need for systematic incorporation of domain-specific knowledge.
AI.method: Compared retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) with knowledge graph (KG)-based methods using clinically validated KGs capturing causal relationships between stressors, interventions, and outcomes, evaluated with BERTScore F1, SBERT cosine similarity, and human evaluation across five metrics.
AI.result: KG-based approaches consistently improved contextual relevance, clinical appropriateness, and practical usability compared to RAG alone, demonstrating structured expert-validated knowledge's critical role in addressing LLM limitations.
AI.conclusion: Structured, clinically validated knowledge graphs are essential for improving LLM performance in counseling tasks, addressing limitations in cultural sensitivity and clinical appropriateness that RAG alone cannot solve.

title: Multigrain-aware Semantic Prototype Scanning and Tri-Token Prompt Learning Embraced High-Order RWKV for Pan-Sharpening
summary: In this work, we propose a Multigrain-aware Semantic Prototype Scanning paradigm for pan-sharpening, built upon a high-order RWKV architecture and a tri-token prompting mechanism derived from semantic clustering. Specifically, our method contains three key components: 1) Multigrain-aware Semantic Prototype Scanning. Although RWKV offers a efficient linear-complexity alternative to Transformers, its conventional bidirectional raster scanning is still semantic-agnostic and prone to positional bias. To address this issue, we introduce a semantic-driven scanning strategy that leverages locality-sensitive hashing to group semantically related regions and construct multi-grain semantic prototypes, enabling context-aware token reordering and more coherent global interaction. 2) Tri-token Prompt Learning. We design a tri-token prompting mechanism consisting of a global token, cluster-derived prototype tokens, and a learnable register token. The global and prototype tokens provide complementary semantic priors for RWKV modeling, while the register token helps suppress noisy and artifact-prone intermediate representations. 3) Invertible Q-Shift. To counteract spatial details, we apply center difference convolution on the value pathway to inject high-frequency information, and introduce an invertible multi-scale Q-shift operation for efficient and lossless feature transformation without parameter-heavy receptive field expansion. Experimental results demonstrate the superiority of our method.

AI.tldr: Multigrain-aware Semantic Prototype Scanning for pan-sharpening using high-order RWKV with tri-token prompting and invertible Q-shift operations.
AI.motivation: Traditional RWKV's bidirectional raster scanning is semantic-agnostic and prone to positional bias, limiting effective global interaction for pan-sharpening tasks.
AI.method: Three key components: 1) Multigrain-aware Semantic Prototype Scanning using locality-sensitive hashing for semantic grouping, 2) Tri-token Prompt Learning with global, prototype, and register tokens, 3) Invertible Q-Shift with center difference convolution for high-frequency injection.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method over existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed paradigm effectively addresses semantic-agnostic scanning limitations in RWKV for pan-sharpening through semantic-driven scanning, tri-token prompting, and efficient feature transformation.

title: ADAPT: Benchmarking Commonsense Planning under Unspecified Affordance Constraints
summary: Intelligent embodied agents should not simply follow instructions, as real-world environments often involve unexpected conditions and exceptions. However, existing methods usually focus on directly executing instructions, without considering whether the target objects can actually be manipulated, meaning they fail to assess available affordances. To address this limitation, we introduce DynAfford, a benchmark that evaluates embodied agents in dynamic environments where object affordances may change over time and are not specified in the instruction. DynAfford requires agents to perceive object states, infer implicit preconditions, and adapt their actions accordingly. To enable this capability, we introduce ADAPT, a plug-and-play module that augments existing planners with explicit affordance reasoning. Experiments demonstrate that incorporating ADAPT significantly improves robustness and task success across both seen and unseen environments. We also show that a domain-adapted, LoRA-finetuned vision-language model used as the affordance inference backend outperforms a commercial LLM (GPT-4o), highlighting the importance of task-aligned affordance grounding.

AI.tldr: DynAfford benchmark tests embodied agents on dynamic environments with changing object affordances, requiring perception of object states and implicit precondition inference. ADAPT module adds affordance reasoning to existing planners, improving robustness.
AI.motivation: Existing embodied agents blindly follow instructions without assessing whether target objects can actually be manipulated (affordances), failing in real-world environments with unexpected conditions and exceptions.
AI.method: Introduces DynAfford benchmark for dynamic environments with changing affordances, and ADAPT plug-and-play module that augments existing planners with explicit affordance reasoning using a domain-adapted, LoRA-finetuned vision-language model.
AI.result: ADAPT significantly improves robustness and task success across both seen and unseen environments. The domain-adapted vision-language model outperforms GPT-4o for affordance inference, showing importance of task-aligned grounding.
AI.conclusion: Explicit affordance reasoning is crucial for robust embodied agents in dynamic environments. Task-aligned affordance grounding outperforms general-purpose LLMs, and the ADAPT module provides a practical solution for enhancing existing planners.

title: Prompt Optimization Is a Coin Flip: Diagnosing When It Helps in Compound AI Systems
summary: Prompt optimization in compound AI systems is statistically indistinguishable from a coin flip: across 72 optimization runs on Claude Haiku (6 methods $\times$ 4 tasks $\times$ 3 repeats), 49% score below zero-shot; on Amazon Nova Lite, the failure rate is even higher. Yet on one task, all six methods improve over zero-shot by up to $+6.8$ points. What distinguishes success from failure? We investigate with 18,000 grid evaluations and 144 optimization runs, testing two assumptions behind end-to-end optimization tools like TextGrad and DSPy: (A) individual prompts are worth optimizing, and (B) agent prompts interact, requiring joint optimization. Interaction effects are never significant ($p > 0.52$, all $F < 1.0$), and optimization helps only when the task has exploitable output structure -- a format the model can produce but does not default to. We provide a two-stage diagnostic: an \$80 ANOVA pre-test for agent coupling, and a 10-minute headroom test that predicts whether optimization is worthwhile -- turning a coin flip into an informed decision.

AI.tldr: Prompt optimization success in compound AI systems is unpredictable (49% fail), but predictable via diagnostic tests: optimization only helps when tasks have exploitable output structure, not from agent interaction effects.
AI.motivation: To understand why prompt optimization in compound AI systems yields inconsistent results (sometimes failing, sometimes succeeding dramatically), and to identify what distinguishes successful from failed optimization attempts.
AI.method: Conducted 18,000 grid evaluations and 144 optimization runs across Claude Haiku and Amazon Nova Lite models, testing six optimization methods on four tasks with three repeats.
